questions.question: Why is URL shortener a useful full-loop drill prompt?
options: It is familiar enough to practice process quality while still exposing real scaling and trade-off choices.
It has no meaningful failure modes, so it is easy to finish quickly.
It removes the need for clarification because everyone defines it the same way.
It only tests storage schema design.
questions.explanation: The prompt is approachable but still rich enough for end-to-end interview practice.

questions.question: What is the biggest risk of using a familiar prompt poorly?
options: You may fall back to memorized components and skip clarification, sizing, and trade-offs.
You will automatically overestimate redirect latency.
The interviewer will forbid caches in the design.
You must choose multi-region writes immediately.
questions.explanation: Familiarity can tempt you into pattern-recitation instead of structured reasoning.

questions.question: Which set of elements best demonstrates full-loop coverage on a URL shortener answer?
options: Clarification, sizing, high-level design, deep dive, failure modes, observability, trade-offs, and redesign.
Architecture diagram, API list, and a favorite database choice.
Only redirect flow and ID generation details.
Only storage schema and cache sizing.
questions.explanation: The drill is meant to exercise the entire interview loop, not just the core architecture.

questions.question: What is often the most important capacity insight for a URL shortener?
options: The redirect path is read-heavy, so cache behavior and hot-key handling matter early.
The create path always dominates total traffic.
The main challenge is full-text indexing of URLs.
Object storage throughput is the first bottleneck.
questions.explanation: Redirect-heavy load usually shapes the architecture more than writes.

questions.question: Which deep dive is strongest when the interviewer emphasizes abuse risk?
options: URL validation, quarantine flow, and how redirects are blocked or degraded safely.
Admin dashboard color themes and moderation tools.
A second reporting database for future exports.
A generic read replica discussion with no abuse controls.
questions.explanation: The deep dive should match the dominant concern.

questions.question: What trade-off does a cache-heavy redirect path introduce?
options: Lower read latency, but more complexity around invalidation and hot-key protection.
Higher write throughput with no additional operational cost.
Perfect consistency with no failure risk.
Less need for observability because the cache hides origin failures.
questions.explanation: Caching helps reads, but creates correctness and operational complexity.

questions.question: Which signal best shows that your full-loop drill answer is improving?
options: You consistently reach redesign and operational wrap-up without losing architecture clarity.
You mention more storage technologies in every rehearsal.
Your diagrams contain more boxes than before.
You spend most of the interview on ID generation.
questions.explanation: Improvement means reaching all stages with coherent trade-offs, not just adding detail.

questions.question: If the interviewer changes the prompt to global multi-region writes, what should you discuss first?
options: How write coordination, deletion semantics, and consistency expectations change across regions.
How to rename the API endpoints to match the new scope.
How to remove caches because they are no longer useful.
How to skip the wrap-up to save time.
questions.explanation: Multi-region writes mainly stress coordination, correctness, and operational complexity.
